---
editor: source
format:
  html:
    toc: true
bibliography: references.bib
link-external-newwindow: true
---


![](logocolour.jpg){width=300}

<font size = 10>Urban analytics and city science</font>
<font size = 6></br>APIs and urban data</font>
<font size = 4></br>[Professor Emmanouil Tranos](https://etranos.info/), (updated: `r format(Sys.Date(), "%b %d %Y")`)</font>

## Introduction

Welcome to the [School of Geographical Sciences](http://www.bristol.ac.uk/geography/), [University of Bristol](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/). The School has a long and distinguished history of teaching quantitative methods, spatial modelling and geographic data science to all of our students. Historically, some of our pioneering staff in this field have included:

![](haggett.jpg){width=100}

[Peter Haggett](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Peter_Haggett), one of the most influential geographers of his generation;

![](johnston.jpg){width=100}

[Ron Johnston](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ron_Johnston_(geographer)), one of the most prolific authors ever in human geography;

![](hepple.jpg){width=100}

[Les Hepple](https://doi.org/10.1068/a4097), an exceptional scholar, polymath and dedicated teacher; and

![](jones.jpg){width=100}

[Kelvyn Jones](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kelvyn_Jones), a leading pioneer of quantitative methods in human geography.

We continue this tradition into the present-day through our [Quantitative Spatial Science Research Group](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/geography/research/quantitative-spatial-science/), which (no longer all male and all white!) leads the teaching of quantitative methods to students in all years of our undergraduate (and Masters) programmes. These include [BSc Geography](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/geography/courses/undergraduate/) and our [MSc in Geographic Data Science and Spatial Analytics](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/study/postgraduate/taught/msc-geographic-data-science-and-spatial-analytics/) and [MSc in Environmental Modelling and Data Analysis](https://www.bristol.ac.uk/study/postgraduate/taught/msc-environmental-modelling-and-data-analysis/).


## Why we teach computing and data-handling skills

Programming languages such as [R](https://cran.r-project.org/) and [Python](https://www.python.org/) provide an integrated environment that can be used to input, analyse and communicate the results of data-based geographic research, bringing together data manipulation, statistical analysis and visual methods such as charts and maps. This webpage was written in [R Studio](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/), allowing me to bring together into one document the computer code, the results of running that code and other text such as what you are reading. More standard 'off-the-shelf' software such as Excel do not allow this. Presently, we teach R to **all our students**. We do this to support their training in research methods -- the sorts of skills that all geographers need [and are expected to have](https://www.qaa.ac.uk/quality-code/subject-benchmark-statements/geography) -- and also to enhance their employability because data science skills are well regarded by employers.

We do believe that a *critical understanding* of the technicalities of data handling and analysis is important for all geographers, even in an era of [vibe coding](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vibe_coding).

## Practicalities for today's exercise

All you need to do for today's session is follow along with the instructor, copying and pasting code from this document into the R Console as they do. **Please do not rush ahead as this is more likely to generate errors that you will then need to go back and correct**. Just take it one step at a time, in company with the instructor. The purpose of the session is not to teach you coding so there is no need to worry about what the code fully means. It is simply to give you a flavour of the sort of skills we teach our students.

If you wish, you could run this practical again from a personal computer when you get home. If you do, take a note of the web address, which is [https://etranos.info/openday](https://etranos.info/openday) and be aware that you will first need to install [R](https://cran.r-project.org/) and then [RStudio Desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/) on the computer for the following code to work. These software are free and operate on Windows, Mac OS and Linux.

## Aims of the exercise

-   Understand the technicalities and the breadth of the urban data landscape

-   Understand the *messiness* of real world (urban) data

-   Access programmatically urban data and understand the benefits of such data access practise.

## APIs

Application Programming Interface (APIs) represent a 'gate' or otherwise a platform that enables a client (that is you) to interact with a server (for example [opendata.bristol.gov.uk](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/)). According to @amazonWhatAPI:

> In the context of APIs, the word Application refers to any software with a distinct function. Interface can be thought of as a contract of service between two applications. This contract defines how the two communicate with each other using requests and responses. Their API documentation contains information on how developers are to structure those requests and responses.

The client's software (this might be R for example) sends a request to the server requesting specific data. The response is the data the client asked.

More commonly, the client might be a mobile phone app (e.g. train network status app) and the server is the network operator's server.

APIs can be private or public types. For more inthe description from @amazonWhatAPI [here](https://aws.amazon.com/what-is/api/#:~:text=API%20stands%20for%20Application%20Programming,other%20using%20requests%20and%20responses.)

## The JSON file format

Although some urban data can be downloaded as a tidy[^1] table in a `.csv` format, most APIs will ship data to you in a `JSON` format.

[^1]: Every column is a variable; every row is an observation; every cell is a single value. See [here])(https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/tidyr/vignettes/tidy-data.html) for more info

According to the official JSON website [@jsonJSON], JSON

> is a lightweight data-interchange format. It is easy for humans to read and write. It is easy for machines to parse and generate. It is based on a subset of the JavaScript Programming Language Standard ECMA-262 3rd Edition - December 1999. JSON is a text format that is completely language independent but uses conventions that are familiar to programmers of the C-family of languages, including C, C++, C#, Java, JavaScript, Perl, Python, and many others. These properties make JSON an ideal data-interchange language.

It adopts a hierarchical structure such as `attribute:value` instead of the fixed structure of tidy data. Following @singleton2017urban, such `attribute:value` pairs might be:

-   "mean income":"50000"

-   "internet speed":"35"

-   "jobs":"15000"

These attributes might be organised hierarchically:

("city":"Bristol,"mean income": "27000", "internet speed": "55", "jobs":("2019":"15000", "2020":"14000")), ("city":"Newcastle,"mean income": "25000", "internet speed": "45", "jobs":("2019":"11000", "2020":"10000"))

Although this format is more verbose (attribute names are repeated) it is more flexible (not all observations need to have all attributes) [@singleton2017urban]

## Practical example with API

[opendata.bristol.gov.uk](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/search) offers a lot of data about Bristol. For this example, let's try to work with [Jobs by Ward (by industry) in Bristol 2020](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/datasets/ca24dc2e210940e0aa9ad3a4785a591b_17/explore). You can also access these data by searching for 'Jobs by industry in Bristol by Ward'. Use the filter icon to filter for 2020 data. As you can see, this is a small data set with $34$ observations representing Bristol's $34$ Wards. You can visualise these data with the `Table` tab on the right hand-side and even download a `.csv` with these data. To begin with, do both of these things.

Then, go to the `More Info` icon, scroll down on the left hand side and click on `I want to use this`. You can find the relevant link under `View API Resources`. We are interested in the `GeoJSON`. You can view the data in this format and also copy the links to access these data *programmatically*.  The *weird* output on your screen is the data you requested in `JSON` format. If you carefully read the URL from right to left you can see some logic on how this URL is built.

```{block, type='alert alert-warning'}

**Question**: Why should you go through all these efforts to build an API request and 
access the data in `JSON` file format instead of just manually downloading a `.csv` file?

```

So, let's try to access these data programmatically. First, we need to load the relevant `R` packages

### To Begin

When the instructor invites you to do so, please open RStudio on your computer.

### Install/load any required libraries

The great advantage of software such as R is that it has available a large number of additional libraries that extend its functionality -- for mapping, for example. The ones we need today are listed below. The code chunk, below, checks to see if these libraries have been installed already, if not then installs them, and then instructs that they are required for the current session in R.


```{r}
#| collapse: true
#| message: false
#| warning: false

if(!"tidyverse" %in% installed.packages()[,1]) install.packages("tidyverse")
if(!"jsonlite" %in% installed.packages()[,1]) install.packages("jsonlite")
if(!"sf" %in% installed.packages()[,1]) install.packages("sf")

require(tidyverse)
require(jsonlite)
require(sf)
```

### Access urban data through an API

To access the data, we are going to use the function `fromJSON()` from the `jsonlite` package. Please check the syntax of this function as well as the other function of this package.

```{r get data old}
#| echo: false
#| eval: false

data <- fromJSON("https://services2.arcgis.com/a4vR8lmmksFixzmB/ArcGIS/rest/services/Jobs_by_Ward_(by_industry)_in_Bristol_2020/FeatureServer/0/query?outFields=*&where=1%3D1&f=geojson", flatten = TRUE)
```

<!-- **Please note** that the above API link is different than the one you can obtain today from [opendata.bristol.gov.uk](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/datasets/ca24dc2e210940e0aa9ad3a4785a591b_17/explore). If you use the most recent, you will need to tweak the code bellow. For the purpose of the practical, please use the link provided in the above chunk of code. This is an example of how ephemeral data structures can be! -->

```{r get data}
data <- jsonlite::fromJSON("https://maps2.bristol.gov.uk/server2/rest/services/ext/ward_profiles/MapServer/17/query?f=json&where=(YEAR%20IN%20('2020'))&outFields=*", flatten = TRUE)
```

We now have an `R` object called `data`. Let's try to see how it looks like

```{r type}
typeof(data)
```

So, this is a list. If you need a reminder for what a list is, see [here](http://adv-r.had.co.nz/Data-structures.html). Now the difficult part. Let's try to figure out the structure of this `JSON` formatted data.

```{r structure}
str(data)
```

Well, it's complicated. Looking on the above output of `list()` the object that looks most useful to us is the `data$features`.

```{block, type='alert alert-warning'}

**Question**: Based on the output of `str()`, why do you think is `data$features` 
the most useful part of `data` for us?

```

And if you want to see how the dataframe `data$features` looks like, the below function will show the first five rows.

```{r head}
glimpse(data$features)
```

### Data manipulation

Looking into the above, I think we should remove the string "attributes." from the column names.

To do this, we will use the below functions from the `dplyr` package, which is part of the [`tidyverse` ecosystem](https://www.tidyverse.org/).

```{r wrangling}
data <- data$features %>% 
  rename_with(~sub("attributes.", "", .))   # ~ introduces a formula: function `sub` substitutes (in other words replaces) the string   

data %>% glimpse()                           # provides a glimpse of the output
```

### Some priliminary analysis

Let's try now to do some analysis based on these data and create some outputs. The below code will create a plot for the distribution of jobs by industry in Central Bristol. The code is quite long but it is not difficult to understand if you take it one step at a time. I have added comments to the code to make it easier to understand.

```{r analysis}
data %>% mutate(total = rowSums(across(c(5:22))),           # total jobs in a ward
               across(c(5:22),                             # select the columns you are interested in
                      .names = "{col}_share") / total) %>% # divide these columns by total to create industry share within ward  
                                                           # and name the new columns as oldname_share
  select(2:3, 24:41) %>%
  pivot_longer(!c(WARD_NAME, WARD_CODE),                        # These are the columns I am 'pivoting'
               names_to = "industry",                      # This is how I will name the column with all the industries ...
               values_to = "jobs_share") %>%               # ... and the column with the jobs percentage
                                                           # Look https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html to 
                                                           # understand the long to wide / wide to long transformations.
  filter(WARD_NAME == "Central") %>%                            # Let's focus on Central Bristol
  ggplot(aes(x=industry, y=jobs_share)) +                  # ... and create a bar chart for it jobs distribution
    geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
  
# to understand the "identity part go on the Details section of the geom_bar documentation:
# https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/ggplot2/versions/1.0.1/topics/geom_bar
    
    scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(angle = 90)) +     # This line rotates the x labels,
    ylab("jobs %")                                         # and here we rename the y axis. 
```

Now, let's do something slightly more interesting. Let's create two plots to compare the job distribution between two Bristol wards, Central and Easton.

```{r comparison}
data %>% mutate(total = rowSums(across(c(5:22))),           # total jobs in a ward
               across(c(5:22),                             # select the columns you are interested in
                      .names = "{col}_share") / total) %>% # divide these columns by total to create industry share within ward  
                                                           # and name the new columns as oldname_share
  select(2:3, 24:41) %>%
  pivot_longer(!c(WARD_NAME, WARD_CODE),                        # These are the columns I am 'pivoting'
               names_to = "industry",                      # This is how I will name the column with all the industries ...
               values_to = "jobs_share") %>%               # ... and the column with the jobs percentage
                                                           # Look https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html to 
                                                           # understand the long to wide / wide to long transformations.
  filter(WARD_NAME == "Central" |
         WARD_NAME == "Easton") %>%                             # Let's focus on Easton and Central Bristol
  ggplot(aes(x=industry, y=jobs_share)) +                  # ... and create a bar chart for it jobs distribution
    geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
    scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(angle = 90)) +     # This line rotates the x labels,
    ylab("jobs %") +                                       # and here we rename the y axis. 
    facet_wrap(vars(WARD_NAME), ncol = 2)                       # facet_wrap creates two graphs based on `ward` and puts one next to each other
```

Obviously the `x` labels are quite ugly. Let's fix them. Check the extra lines in the code below.

```{r comparison with nicer labels}
data %>% mutate(total = rowSums(across(c(5:22))),           # total jobs in a ward
               across(c(5:22),                             # select the columns you are interested in
                      .names = "{col}_share") / total) %>% # divide these columns by total to create industry share within ward  
                                                           # and name the new columns as oldname_share
  select(2:3, 24:41) %>%
  pivot_longer(!c(WARD_NAME, WARD_CODE),                   # These are the columns I am 'pivoting'
               names_to = "industry",                      # This is how I will name the column with all the industries ...
               values_to = "jobs_share") %>%               # ... and the column with the jobs percentage
                                                           # Look https://tidyr.tidyverse.org/articles/pivot.html to 
                                                           # understand the long to wide / wide to long transformations.
  
  mutate(industry =  gsub("_share", "", industry)) %>%     # This line removes the "_share" part from the industry names,
  mutate(industry =  sub("__.*", "", industry)) %>%        # ... and this drops everything after the first double underscore (if it exists)
  mutate(industry =  gsub("_", " ", industry)) %>%         # ... and this replaces _ with spaces.
  mutate(industry = str_to_lower(industry)) %>%            # This line makes all industry names lower case
                                                           
  filter(WARD_NAME == "Central" |
         WARD_NAME == "Easton") %>%                        # Let's focus on Easton and Central Bristol
  ggplot(aes(x=industry, y=jobs_share)) +                  # ... and create a bar chart for it jobs distribution
    geom_bar(stat = "identity") +
    scale_x_discrete(guide = guide_axis(angle = 90)) +     # This line rotates the x labels,
    ylab("jobs %") +                                       # and here we rename the y axis. 
    facet_wrap(vars(WARD_NAME), ncol = 2)                  # facet_wrap creates two graphs based on `ward` and puts one next to each other
```

### Do your first map!

What is a geographer without a map? Let's create a map of the total number of jobs in each ward. 

To do this, we need to first download the spatial information for Bristol's wards. We can do this with the `st_read()` function from the `sf` package.
Again, this is freely available from [opendata.bristol.gov.uk](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/datasets/793b569f6f56442bb9e6761f249edd90_4/explore) and you can find the relevant link under `View API Resources` as `GeoJSON`. 

```{r}
bristol.wards <- st_read("https://maps2.bristol.gov.uk/server2/rest/services/ext/ll_boundaries/MapServer/4/query?outFields=*&where=1%3D1&f=geojson")
```

Then, we need to merge the `bristol.wards` spatial dataframe that contains the spatial information for Bristol's wards
`data` dataframe that contains all the job data. We can do this with the `left_join()` function from the `dplyr` package and then create a map.

```{r}
left_join(bristol.wards, data, by = c("WARD_ID" = "WARD_CODE")) %>% 
  mutate(total_jobs = rowSums(across(c(10:27)))) %>% # create a new column with the total number of jobs in each ward
  ggplot() +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = total_jobs)) +               # create a choropleth map with the total number of jobs in each ward
    scale_fill_viridis_c() +                        # use a nice colour palette
    theme_void()                                    # remove axes and background
```

Well done! You've just utilised Bristol's open data and became part of the urban open data revolution 😃.

```{block, type='alert alert-warning'}
**On your own time:** Search for some other interesting data from 
[opendata.bristol.gov.uk](https://opendata.bristol.gov.uk/),
access it programmatically, do the necessary data wrangling and produce some 
graphs in a new `.qmd` document. Make sure to knit it as `.html`.
```

## Accessing APIs using existing `R` packages

Now you might be lucky and somebody else has already done all the infrastructural / back-end work in order for you to easily interrogate an API and access their data. How would this look like? Like another `R` package. Here is a list with a few examples:

-   `nomisr` is a package that allows you to access [Nomis](https://www.nomisweb.co.uk/) data with `R`. Find [here](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/nomisr/vignettes/introduction.html) an introduction.

-   `tidysencus` is part of the `tidyverse` ecosystem and offers access to the US Census. This is the [link](https://walker-data.com/tidycensus/). Pay extra attention to the *spatial data* option.

-   The city of Toronto offers an `R` [package](https://sharlagelfand.github.io/opendatatoronto/) to access [City of Toronto Open Data Portal](https://open.toronto.ca/).

-   See a relevant book by @apis available [here](https://bookdown.org/paul/apis_for_social_scientists/).

## Contingency plan

Some times, APIs are down. So, if this happens during the practical, please use the below lines of code to load the relevant data. Make sure to replace them in the relevant code chunks.

data <- fromJSON("https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/9abs2tn878zwyfaqb80ns/bristol_jobs.json?rlkey=gpd3pc5cw4zs2gypcnk1nvdxp&dl=1", flatten = TRUE)

bristol.wards <- st_read("https://www.dropbox.com/scl/fi/72yd1rkb8qbgvl0l7uree/bristol_wards.geojson?rlkey=t2nyuu1jrkx4a2bpb4eri0idi&dl=1")

## References
